entities.name: āgama
entities.cite: tradition — their jnana too is agama-purvaka, preceded by what was handed down

entities.name: dharma
entities.cite: Dharma is not established by reasoning apart from tradition

entities.name: ṛṣi
entities.cite: the seers whose authority rests on direct vision

entities.name: jñāna
entities.cite: the knowledge that the seers possess

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: The first line subordinates tarka to agama in matters of dharma

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: their jnana too is agama-purvaka, preceded by what was handed down

axioms.name: samvit
axioms.cite: rsis, whose authority rests on direct vision

denials.name: svatantra
denials.cite: The first line subordinates tarka to agama in matters of dharma

denials.name: aparoksa
denials.cite: Every seeing is already informed by what was received

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: not established by reasoning apart from tradition

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: the knowledge that the seers possess is itself preceded by tradition

rejected_readings.label: tarka_sufficient
rejected_readings.rendering: Reasoning alone is sufficient to establish dharma.
rejected_readings.why: The verse explicitly negates this: dharma is not established by reasoning apart from tradition. Tarka is not self-sufficient; it depends on agama.

rejected_readings.label: rsi_unmediated_knowledge
rejected_readings.rendering: The seers possess direct, unmediated knowledge of truth.
rejected_readings.why: The verse contradicts this in its second line: even rsi-jnana is agama-purvaka, preceded by tradition. The commentary emphasizes: 'There is no such unmediated layer.'

notes: The contract captures the verse's two-part subordination of tarka and rsi-knowledge to agama. This creates an acknowledged circularity: tradition is validated by seers whose authority is constituted by tradition. The absolute sort for rsi reflects their foundational epistemic status.
